  • Have not previously received support under the relevant Recovery and Resilience Plan scholarship scheme

Previous Scholarship Recipients

Students who previously received support under the earlier Recovery and Resilience Plan scholarship scheme at bachelor’s level may qualify if they:

  • Successfully complete their bachelor’s degree
  • Progress continuously to an eligible master’s programme

For master’s applicants, previous study at a Slovak higher education institution does not automatically disqualify the applicant.

Citizenship and Residence Requirements

Applicants must normally hold citizenship of a country other than Slovakia.

Citizenship must be demonstrated through an acceptable identity document, generally:

  • A valid passport
  • A national identity card for eligible citizens of the European Union, European Economic Area or Switzerland

Dual citizens who hold both Slovak citizenship and another citizenship may apply under the stated rules.

Slovaks living abroad may also qualify, but they must submit the appropriate certificate issued under Slovak legislation concerning Slovaks living abroad.

Applicants must also demonstrate that they have not held permanent residence in Slovakia at any time from 1 January 2021 until the date of submitting the scholarship application.

This condition is normally confirmed through a hand-signed solemn declaration.

Temporary residence in Slovakia may not automatically make an applicant ineligible. The published announcement specifically notes that some holders of temporary residence permits may still qualify, depending on the programme’s conditions and exceptions.

Admission to a Slovak University

The scholarship application and university admission process are separate.

Receiving the scholarship does not automatically mean that the applicant has been admitted to a university. Similarly, applying to a university does not automatically produce a scholarship application.

Applicants must therefore complete two distinct processes:

  1. Apply directly to an eligible Slovak higher education institution for admission.
  2. Submit a separate online application for the scholarship.

Applicants should communicate with their preferred university as early as possible to confirm:

  • Whether the programme is supported
  • Admission requirements
  • Required academic documents
  • Language requirements
  • Tuition fees
  • Recognition of previous qualifications
  • Enrolment dates
  • Programme-specific deadlines

The official scholarship information strongly advises applicants to contact their selected institution early to avoid delays or misunderstandings.

Standardised Test Requirement

The selection process is based on the applicant’s results in an approved standardised test.

Applicants must submit results from either:

  • The SAT, administered by the College Board; or
  • The SCIO Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites tests

The standardised-test result is described as the only criterion used to determine the ranking of eligible applicants.

Documents such as the CV and motivation letter are still mandatory, but applicants who do not meet the required test threshold will not proceed in the selection process.

Minimum SAT Score

Applicants submitting SAT results must achieve:

  • A minimum total SAT score of 1,100 points
  • Equivalent to approximately the 63rd percentile under the programme’s stated system

SAT results from tests taken between 1 January 2021 and the application deadline may be accepted.

The announcement identified 6 June 2026 as the latest relevant SAT date for the current cycle. Applicants applying close to the July deadline would therefore generally need to have already taken the examination and obtained an eligible result.

Minimum SCIO Score

Applicants using SCIO results must take both:

  • Mathematics, known as MAT
  • General Academic Prerequisites, known as VŠP

The applicant must achieve an average percentile of at least 63 across the two tests.

The application system calculates the average using the percentile achieved in each examination.

Applicants must submit an official SCIO certificate. One SCIO test alone is not sufficient because both Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites are required.

Selection Process

After the application deadline, the Ministry of Education, Research, Development and Youth of the Slovak Republic or the relevant higher education institutions will verify submitted applications.

The assessment may include confirmation of:

  • Citizenship
  • Residence history
  • Educational background
  • University application or admission
  • Eligibility of the selected programme
  • Full-time study status
  • Standardised-test score
  • Completeness and authenticity of documents
  • Compliance with all declarations and requirements

Eligible applicants will be ranked according to their accepted SAT or SCIO results.

Applicants below the minimum threshold will not be considered. Meeting the minimum score does not guarantee selection because the number of scholarships is limited and the applicant’s ranking among other eligible candidates will also matter.

Preliminary and final lists of successful applicants will be prepared after verification and enrolment procedures.

Scholarship payments begin only after the student has:

  1. Been officially admitted
  2. Enrolled at the selected institution
  3. Commenced the eligible study programme
  4. Completed the required administrative process

Where administrative delays occur, eligible payments may be made retroactively for the months during which the student qualified.

Documents Required for the Application

Applicants should prepare the following documents.

1. Identity Document

Applicants must provide a certified copy of a valid passport or another accepted identity document.

2. Declaration of Permanent Residence

A hand-signed solemn declaration must confirm that the applicant has not held permanent residence in Slovakia between 1 January 2021 and the application deadline.

3. Certificate for Slovaks Living Abroad

This is required only for applicants applying under the special eligibility provisions for Slovaks living abroad.

4. Legal Guardian’s Consent

Applicants under 18 must submit a hand-signed consent form from their legal guardian concerning the processing of personal data.

5. SAT or SCIO Certificate

Applicants must provide proof of:

  • An SAT score of at least 1,100; or
  • An average SCIO percentile of at least 63 across Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites

6. University Acceptance Letter

Applicants should upload an acceptance letter from a Slovak higher education institution where available.

The published rules indicate that an applicant may still apply for the scholarship after applying to the university but before receiving final admission. Nevertheless, official admission and enrolment will be required before the scholarship can be awarded and paid.

7. Curriculum Vitae

A structured CV in English is mandatory. The official template should be used where required by the application portal.

8. Motivation Letter

Applicants must submit a motivation letter in English using the official template.

The motivation letter should explain:

  • Why the applicant wants to study in Slovakia
  • Why the selected programme is appropriate
  • How the applicant demonstrates talent
  • What the applicant hopes to achieve academically and professionally

Both the CV and motivation letter are described as essential application documents.

How to Apply

Applications must be submitted electronically through the official scholarship portal.

Applications cannot be submitted by email.

A complete application should include:

  • Personal and citizenship information
  • Education details
  • Information about the chosen Slovak institution and programme
  • SAT or SCIO results
  • Certified identity documentation
  • Required solemn declarations
  • CV
  • Motivation letter
  • Supporting documents applicable to the applicant’s circumstances

Applicants should not wait until the final day. The application may require certified documents, signatures, test certificates and information from a university, all of which may take time to organise.

The active application announcement states that registration is open until 31 July 2026.

APPLY HERE

Application Deadline

The deadline for the Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students is:

31 July 2026 at 23:59 Central European Summer Time

Applicants should confirm the time-zone difference in their country and submit well before the portal closes.

Because the source material contains both an active application notice and older wording that registration had not yet opened, applicants should rely on the current online portal status and verify that the submission function is active before preparing a last-minute application.

Tuition Fees

The scholarship does not create an automatic universal tuition-fee waiver.

Students admitted to a full-time foreign-language programme at a public Slovak higher education institution may have their tuition fees waived under the relevant Slovak legislation. However, the final decision belongs to the individual higher education institution.

Applicants should therefore ask their chosen university:

  • Whether tuition fees apply
  • Whether scholarship recipients qualify for a waiver
  • Whether the waiver covers the full tuition amount
  • Whether any administrative or programme fees remain payable

A scholarship applicant should not assume that receiving €500 per month also guarantees free tuition.

Health Insurance

Scholarship recipients may qualify for public health insurance in Slovakia under the applicable legislation where they:

  • Do not have permanent residence in Slovakia
  • Are not already insured in another European Union member state, European Economic Area contracting state or Switzerland
  • Hold a scholarship under a programme approved by the Slovak Government

Applicants should verify their individual insurance position before travelling, particularly because insurance arrangements may differ according to citizenship, residence status and existing coverage.

Visa and Temporary Residence Requirements

Non-EU students may need a national visa and temporary residence permit for study.

Applicants currently outside Slovakia are advised to apply for a national Type D visa through the Slovak embassy or an authorised external service provider responsible for their country of residence.

The published guidance recommends applying for a 120-day national D visa connected to a temporary-residence application for study.

After arriving in Slovakia, students must apply for temporary residence for study within the permitted period.

Students already legally present in Slovakia may be able to apply through a Foreign Police office.

The scholarship does not remove the applicant’s responsibility to satisfy immigration, visa and residence requirements. Applicants should begin these procedures early, because university enrolment and scholarship eligibility may be affected by delays.

Conditions for Keeping the Scholarship

Successful applicants must remain enrolled in an eligible full-time programme.

The scholarship may be lost where the student:

  • Terminates the supported study programme
  • Transfers to a different higher education institution
  • Changes from full-time to part-time or external study
  • Repeats a study year in circumstances not permitted by the programme
  • Loses student status
  • Interrupts studies beyond the permitted period
  • Submits false or altered documents
  • Fails to comply with inspections connected to the scholarship

A student may be allowed to change to another eligible programme within the same institution if the studies remain continuous and the student does not lose their status, restart the first year or repeat an academic year.

The scholarship cannot normally be transferred from one university to another.

Important Application Advice

Applicants should take the following steps before applying:

  1. Confirm that the chosen university is a public or state Slovak institution.
  2. Check that the selected programme appears on the eligible Smart Specialisation list.
  3. Apply separately for university admission.
  4. Confirm whether the programme is full-time and conducted in person.
  5. Verify that an eligible SAT or SCIO result is available.
  6. Prepare certified identity documents.
  7. Complete all required declarations by hand where instructed.
  8. Prepare the English CV and motivation letter.
  9. Confirm tuition-fee arrangements with the university.
  10. Research visa, temporary residence and health-insurance requirements.
  11. Submit the scholarship application before 31 July 2026.
  12. Keep copies of every document and submission confirmation.

Applicants should also remember that reaching the minimum test score does not guarantee an award. Selection remains competitive because applicants are ranked and the number of available scholarships is limited.

Conclusion

The Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students 2026/27 provide a substantial opportunity for international students seeking to pursue higher education in the heart of Europe.

With funding of €500 per month, eligible bachelor’s and combined-degree students may receive up to €15,000, while eligible master’s students may receive up to €10,000. The programme is available across selected academic fields and public or state higher education institutions in Slovakia.

However, the application process is demanding. Applicants must satisfy citizenship and residence conditions, choose an eligible study programme, apply separately to a Slovak university, submit a valid SAT or SCIO result, prepare all required documents and complete the online application before the deadline.

Interested students should begin by confirming programme eligibility and reviewing their standardised-test results. A complete application must be submitted by 31 July 2026 at 23:59 CEST.

For more information about this opportunity, click here.

Discover more global job opportunities, click here.

The post Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students 2026/27: Study in Europe and Receive Up to €15,000 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: ICEGOV 2026 Scholarships: Apply for Free Conference Registration and a $300 Grant to Attend the International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance
item.dc:creator: Nana Safo
item.description:
The International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance (ICEGOV) 2026 has officially opened applications for its scholarship programme, offering accepted paper authors the opportunity to attend one of the world’s leading conferences on digital governance with financial support. Organized by the United Nations University Operating Unit on Policy-Driven Electronic Governance (UNU-EGOV), the ICEGOV […]

The post ICEGOV 2026 Scholarships: Apply for Free Conference Registration and a $300 Grant to Attend the International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance (ICEGOV) 2026 has officially opened applications for its scholarship programme, offering accepted paper authors the opportunity to attend one of the world’s leading conferences on digital governance with financial support.

Organized by the United Nations University Operating Unit on Policy-Driven Electronic Governance (UNU-EGOV), the ICEGOV Scholarship Programme is designed to increase participation from students and researchers, particularly those from emerging economies. The initiative helps reduce financial barriers, enabling scholars to present their research, engage with global experts, and strengthen their academic and professional networks.

Since its introduction in 2014, the programme has awarded approximately 300 scholarships to researchers from 56 countries, with about 85% of recipients coming from low-, lower-middle-, and upper-middle-income economies.

Applications are currently open and must be submitted by 24 July 2026.

What is the ICEGOV Scholarship Programme?

The ICEGOV Scholarship Programme supports authors whose research papers have been accepted for presentation at ICEGOV 2026.

The scholarship aims to encourage broader global participation by assisting researchers who may otherwise face financial challenges in attending the conference.

Successful applicants receive support that allows them to participate in discussions on electronic governance, digital transformation, public policy innovation, sustainable development, and citizen engagement while presenting their own research before an international audience.

About ICEGOV

The International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance (ICEGOV) is one of the world’s most respected conferences focusing on Digital Governance.

Established in 2007, ICEGOV serves as an international platform where:

  • Researchers present cutting-edge studies.
  • Governments share policy innovations.
  • International organizations exchange best practices.
  • Industry leaders discuss technological advancements.
  • Civil society organizations contribute practical perspectives on governance.

The conference has been hosted in 16 cities across 15 countries on six continents, reflecting its truly global reach.

Today, ICEGOV is coordinated by the United Nations University Operating Unit on Policy-Driven Electronic Governance (UNU-EGOV), headquartered in Guimarães, Portugal.

UNU-EGOV functions as a policy-oriented think tank within the United Nations system, focusing on:

  • Digital Governance
  • Sustainable Development
  • Public Sector Innovation
  • Social Inclusion
  • Active Citizenship
  • Evidence-Based Policymaking

Scholarship Benefits

Selected applicants will receive:

  • Free registration for ICEGOV 2026.
  • A lump-sum financial support of USD $300.

The scholarship is intended to reduce participation costs and make attendance more accessible for researchers from developing and emerging economies.

Expenses Not Covered

Applicants should note that the scholarship does not cover:

  • International airfare
  • Local transportation
  • Accommodation
  • Visa expenses
  • Personal expenses during the conference

Recipients are expected to finance these costs independently.

Because travel arrangements and visa processing can take considerable time, applicants are encouraged to begin preparations as early as possible after their papers have been accepted.

Who Will Receive Priority?

As scholarship funding is limited, preference will be given to applicants who satisfy both of the following conditions:

  1. They are enrolled as students during the 2025/26 academic year.
  2. They are citizens or residents of countries classified by the World Bank as:
    • Low-income economies
    • Lower-middle-income economies
    • Upper-middle-income economies

Applicants meeting both conditions have the strongest likelihood of selection.

Student Eligibility Requirements

For scholarship purposes, a student is defined as someone officially enrolled in one of the following programmes during the 2025/26 academic year:

  • Bachelor’s Degree
  • Master’s Degree
  • Doctoral (PhD) Degree

Applicants enrolled in postdoctoral programmes are not considered students under this scholarship policy.

Student applicants must also submit proof of enrolment in English (PDF format) by email to the conference organizers to verify their eligibility.

Scholarship Payment Conditions

The USD $300 grant is subject to attendance requirements.

Recipients must:

  • Attend the conference in person.
  • Participate throughout all four conference days.
  • Attend the Doctoral Colloquium if their accepted paper is scheduled for that session.

The financial support will be transferred to the recipient’s bank account after the conference.

Applicants should note that payment processing may take up to two months following the event.

Required Information for the Scholarship Application

Applicants will be required to provide the following details:

  • Full name (matching passport or national ID)
  • Email address
  • Gender (for statistical purposes only)
  • Country of residence
  • Nationality
  • Institutional affiliation and country
  • Accepted paper title and paper ID
  • Student status
  • Scholarship motivation statement explaining why financial assistance is needed

Student applicants must additionally email proof of enrolment for the 2025/26 academic year.

Important Application Rules

Applicants should carefully follow the scholarship guidelines.

Key rules include:

  • Submit only one scholarship application.
  • If multiple applications are submitted, only the most recent submission will be considered.
  • Only one author from each accepted paper may apply.
  • If multiple co-authors of the same paper submit scholarship applications, all related applications may be disqualified.

Following these rules carefully is essential to avoid losing eligibility.

Why You Should Apply

Receiving an ICEGOV Scholarship offers numerous academic and professional advantages.

These include:

  • Presenting your research at an internationally recognized conference.
  • Networking with researchers, policymakers, government officials, and international organizations.
  • Learning about the latest developments in Digital Governance and Electronic Governance.
  • Gaining international visibility for your research.
  • Building collaborations for future academic and professional opportunities.
  • Receiving financial support that reduces conference participation costs.

For students and early-career researchers, the programme provides valuable exposure to the global research community while enhancing academic development.

APPLY HERE

Application Deadline

Deadline: 24 July 2026

Applicants are strongly encouraged to complete their applications well before the deadline and ensure all required documents, including proof of enrolment (where applicable), are submitted correctly.

For more scholarships, visit the OFY website HERE

Conclusion

The ICEGOV 2026 Scholarship Programme represents an outstanding opportunity for researchers and students whose papers have been accepted for presentation at one of the world’s leading conferences on digital governance. By providing free conference registration and a USD $300 financial contribution, the programme helps make international academic participation more accessible, particularly for scholars from emerging economies. If you meet the eligibility requirements and have an accepted paper, submitting a strong scholarship application before 24 July 2026 could significantly enhance your opportunity to participate in this prestigious global event while expanding your professional network and contributing to discussions shaping the future of electronic governance.

The post ICEGOV 2026 Scholarships: Apply for Free Conference Registration and a $300 Grant to Attend the International Conference on Theory and Practice of Electronic Governance appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026: Free Online Learning Opportunity for Young Leaders Tackling Global Existential Threats
item.dc:creator: Bob Waudo
item.description:
As the world faces increasingly interconnected challenges such as climate change, artificial intelligence risks, global health emergencies, conflicts, and environmental degradation, there is a growing need for a new generation of leaders capable of addressing these complex issues through collaboration and long-term thinking. To help prepare young leaders for this responsibility, the One Young World […]

The post One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026: Free Online Learning Opportunity for Young Leaders Tackling Global Existential Threats appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
As the world faces increasingly interconnected challenges such as climate change, artificial intelligence risks, global health emergencies, conflicts, and environmental degradation, there is a growing need for a new generation of leaders capable of addressing these complex issues through collaboration and long-term thinking. To help prepare young leaders for this responsibility, the One Young World Academy has launched an innovative online learning programme titled Introduction to Planetary Leadership.

Developed with the sponsorship of the InterAction Council, this globally recognized e-learning course introduces participants to the concept of planetary leadership, explores humanity’s most pressing existential threats, and presents Strategy X-EXIST, a comprehensive framework designed to safeguard the future of humanity.

The programme offers participants expert-led instruction, practical leadership insights, and an internationally recognized digital certificate upon successful completion. It is specifically designed for professionals, community leaders, policymakers, entrepreneurs, NGO workers, and young changemakers who are passionate about creating sustainable solutions to global challenges.

Whether you work in climate action, public health, peacebuilding, technology governance, education, public service, or community development, this free online course provides valuable knowledge and practical tools to help you become a more effective global leader.

About the One Young World Academy

The One Young World Academy is an internationally recognized digital learning platform that equips young leaders with knowledge, leadership skills, and practical solutions to solve some of the world’s biggest challenges.

Through expert-led courses, participants gain access to world-class educational content developed by internationally respected organizations and thought leaders.

The Introduction to Planetary Leadership course expands this mission by helping learners understand how today’s interconnected global crises require coordinated international leadership and innovative thinking.

The programme emphasizes collaboration between governments, scientists, civil society organizations, businesses, communities, and young people to collectively build a safer and more sustainable future.

What is Planetary Leadership?

Planetary leadership is a leadership model that encourages people to think beyond national borders and individual sectors.

Rather than solving problems independently, planetary leadership recognizes that global issues are interconnected and require cooperation among multiple stakeholders.

The course demonstrates how young leaders can contribute to building resilient societies capable of responding to existential threats through collaboration, responsible governance, innovation, and long-term planning.

Participants will learn why leadership in the modern world must focus not only on immediate challenges but also on protecting future generations.

Understanding Strategy X-EXIST

At the center of this course is Strategy X-EXIST, a comprehensive action plan developed by the InterAction Council.

The strategy focuses on preventing and reducing existential risks through four interconnected pillars:

1. Planet

This pillar addresses environmental sustainability and climate security.

Participants explore topics including:

  • Climate change
  • Environmental degradation
  • Biodiversity protection
  • Ecological resilience
  • Sustainable development
  • Climate adaptation
  • Environmental governance

2. People

This section examines issues affecting human well-being, including:

  • Global health
  • Pandemic preparedness
  • Human security
  • Social resilience
  • Public health systems
  • Equity and inclusion

3. Peace

Participants learn about:

  • Conflict prevention
  • Peacebuilding
  • International cooperation
  • Governance
  • Security policy
  • Diplomacy
  • Community resilience

4. Prosperity

The final pillar focuses on creating sustainable economies through:

  • Responsible technology
  • Ethical artificial intelligence
  • Innovation
  • Economic resilience
  • Inclusive development
  • Digital governance

Why Planetary Leadership Matters

Today’s global challenges rarely exist in isolation.

For example:

  • Climate change affects food security.
  • Food insecurity contributes to migration.
  • Migration influences political stability.
  • Political instability affects public health.
  • Artificial intelligence impacts employment, governance, and cybersecurity simultaneously.

The course teaches participants how these issues reinforce one another and why integrated solutions are essential.

Instead of treating problems separately, learners discover systems thinking approaches that produce lasting change.

What You Will Learn

Participants will gain knowledge in several important areas, including:

  • Understanding existential threats facing humanity
  • Principles of planetary leadership
  • Systems thinking
  • Global governance
  • Climate resilience
  • Pandemic preparedness
  • Responsible AI governance
  • Peacebuilding strategies
  • International cooperation
  • Intergenerational leadership
  • Community resilience
  • Advocacy techniques
  • Strategic leadership
  • Risk reduction frameworks

Expert-Led Learning Experience

The programme brings together distinguished experts from the InterAction Council alongside internationally recognized specialists working on global security challenges.

Participants will hear from:

  • InterAction Council representatives
  • Leading climate experts
  • Public health professionals
  • Peacebuilding specialists
  • Artificial intelligence governance experts
  • Young global leaders actively working on existential challenges

One of the highlights is an introductory address by Dr. Jo Nurse, who discusses existential security threats and explains the importance of planetary leadership in today’s world.

The module also includes a keynote presentation explaining the complete Strategy X-EXIST framework.

Course Structure

The programme consists of one comprehensive learning module:

Global and Planetary Security – Prevent and Reduce Risks from Existential Threats

Approximate duration:

  • 45 minutes

The module includes:

  • Video lectures
  • Expert presentations
  • Keynote sessions
  • Action reading materials
  • Practical leadership insights
  • Real-world case studies
  • Interactive learning resources
  • Short assessment quiz

The assessment helps participants evaluate their understanding of the course concepts before completing the programme.

Topics Covered During the Course

Throughout the module, learners explore:

  • Existential security threats
  • Global governance
  • Planetary leadership
  • Climate tipping points
  • Pandemic preparedness
  • Artificial intelligence governance
  • Peacebuilding
  • Systems thinking
  • Advocacy strategies
  • Leadership development
  • Intergenerational collaboration
  • Responsible innovation
  • Community resilience
  • Global cooperation

Benefits of Joining the Programme

Participants receive numerous valuable benefits, including:

Learn from Global Experts

Gain direct insights from internationally respected experts and policymakers working to address humanity’s greatest challenges.

Practical Leadership Skills

Develop actionable strategies that can be implemented within your workplace, organization, community, or country.

Internationally Recognized Certificate

Upon successful completion, participants receive a verified digital certificate issued by the One Young World Academy.

The certificate can be:

  • Added to your CV
  • Shared on LinkedIn
  • Included in professional portfolios
  • Used to demonstrate commitment to global leadership

Guaranteed Access to an Additional MOOC

Participants also receive guaranteed access to the Planetary Leadership Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) developed by the InterAction Council, allowing them to continue expanding their knowledge after completing the introductory programme.

Expand Professional Knowledge

Gain a deeper understanding of:

  • Climate governance
  • Global health
  • AI regulation
  • International cooperation
  • Sustainable leadership
  • Policy development

Who Should Apply?

The programme is intended for individuals who are already making positive contributions within their communities and organizations.

Ideal applicants include:

  • Young professionals
  • Government officials
  • Civil servants
  • Community organizers
  • NGO professionals
  • Social entrepreneurs
  • Business leaders
  • Researchers
  • Development practitioners
  • Climate advocates
  • Peacebuilders
  • Technology professionals
  • Public health workers
  • Policymakers
  • Youth leaders

APPLY HERE

Characteristics of Strong Applicants

The course is particularly suitable for people who:

  • Are actively creating positive change.
  • Work on complex social or environmental challenges.
  • Want to understand how global issues are interconnected.
  • Value fairness, sustainability, and future generations.
  • Prefer practical solutions over theoretical discussions.
  • Have opportunities to influence positive change.
  • Want to promote responsible technology.
  • Care about ethical leadership.
  • Seek sustainable leadership practices that prevent burnout.

Why This Course Stands Out

Unlike many leadership programmes that focus on individual sectors, this course adopts an interdisciplinary approach.

Participants learn how climate, health, technology, peace, governance, and economic systems interact, allowing them to develop more effective and sustainable solutions.

The programme also emphasizes long-term thinking, ethical leadership, and collaborative action, which are increasingly important in today’s interconnected world.

Visit HERE to learn more about the One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026

Application Deadline

Interested applicants should submit their registration before:

Application Deadline: August 7

Applicants are encouraged to register as early as possible to secure their place.

Conclusion

The One Young World Academy – Introduction to Planetary Leadership offers an outstanding opportunity for emerging and experienced leaders to strengthen their understanding of today’s most pressing global challenges. Through expert instruction, practical leadership tools, and the innovative Strategy X-EXIST framework, participants will gain valuable knowledge to help create resilient communities and contribute to a safer, more sustainable future.

Whether your work focuses on climate action, public health, peacebuilding, artificial intelligence, community development, or public policy, this course provides practical insights that can strengthen your leadership and expand your impact. With a verified digital certificate, access to additional learning opportunities, and guidance from internationally recognized experts, this programme represents an excellent investment in your personal and professional development.

Those interested should complete their registration before the August 7 deadline to take advantage of this unique global learning opportunity.

For more global short courses opportunities visit OFY HERE

The post One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026: Free Online Learning Opportunity for Young Leaders Tackling Global Existential Threats appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: United Nations Fund for Population Activities (UNFPA): APRO Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence Partnership Call 2026
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
Background of the UNFPA Asia-Pacific Regional Programme (APRO) UNFPA is launching a new Asia and Pacific Regional Programme covering the period from 2026 to 2029. The programme is aligned with the UNFPA Strategic Plan for the same period and the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development. The regional programme is […]

The post United Nations Fund for Population Activities (UNFPA): APRO Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence Partnership Call 2026 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Background of the UNFPA Asia-Pacific Regional Programme (APRO)

UNFPA is launching a new Asia and Pacific Regional Programme covering the period from 2026 to 2029. The programme is aligned with the UNFPA Strategic Plan for the same period and the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development.

The regional programme is intended to accelerate progress toward UNFPA’s three transformative results:

  1. Ending the unmet need for family planning
  2. Ending preventable maternal deaths
  3. Ending gender-based violence and harmful practices

The programme also seeks to strengthen responses to demographic change while ensuring that no one is left behind. This includes promoting accessible, inclusive and rights-based programming for persons with disabilities and other populations facing exclusion.

Its implementation covers the humanitarian-development-peace continuum, recognizing that sexual and reproductive health, gender equality and protection needs must be addressed in development settings, emergencies and fragile or conflict-affected environments.

Why UNFPA Is Prioritizing Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence

Technological innovation and digitalization have created important opportunities to improve access to services, information, education and communication. However, digital platforms have also become environments in which violence against women and girls can be committed, intensified or extended.

UNFPA’s approach recognizes an online-offline continuum. This means that violence occurring through digital technologies cannot always be separated from violence occurring in homes, workplaces, schools, communities or public spaces.

Technology-facilitated abuse may affect survivors’ safety, privacy, dignity, access to services and participation in public life. Effective programming therefore requires more than isolated online-safety activities. It demands coordinated work involving survivor-centred services, legal and policy systems, digital security, technology design, evidence, ethical data management and prevention.

UNFPA APRO is seeking partners that can contribute specialized knowledge and operational capacity to strengthen both the prevention of TFGBV and the response available to survivors across Asia and the Pacific.

Purpose of the UNFPA Expression of Interest

The purpose of the call is to identify qualified organizations that can collaborate with UNFPA APRO in delivering the Asia-Pacific Regional Programme.

Selected partners may support activities involving:

  • Specialized technical assistance
  • Capacity-strengthening and professional training
  • Survivor-centred support services
  • Prevention and response programming
  • Digital safety and security
  • Legal and policy advice
  • Research and evidence generation
  • Monitoring and evaluation
  • Technology-sector engagement
  • Evidence-based advocacy
  • Innovation and service delivery

Eligible entities selected through the process will sign an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO. This agreement will regulate the technical collaboration and partnership arrangement.

Subject to the availability of funds, UNFPA APRO will subsequently work with successful implementing partners to agree on a work plan and enter into a funded collaboration. Selection through the Expression of Interest process therefore creates a potential pathway to funded implementation, but the announcement does not guarantee funding or state a predetermined grant amount.

Organizations Invited to Submit an Expression of Interest

UNFPA APRO is inviting Expressions of Interest from several categories of legally registered entities.

Academic and research institutions

Universities, research centres, think tanks and other academic institutions may apply where they possess relevant expertise in areas such as:

  • Gender-based violence
  • Digital safety
  • Technology and society
  • Public policy
  • Human rights
  • Law and justice
  • Social research
  • Monitoring and evaluation
  • Statistical measurement
  • Data systems
  • Survivor-centred programming

Academic applicants should demonstrate not only theoretical expertise but also their capacity to translate research into practical programming, policy advice, training or evidence-based advocacy.

Civil society organizations

Eligible civil society applicants include local and international NGOs, women-led organizations, youth-led organizations, civil society networks and rights-based organizations.

Such organizations may be particularly relevant where they have direct experience working with:

  • Survivors of gender-based violence
  • Women and girls in diverse circumstances
  • Young people
  • Frontline responders
  • Human rights defenders
  • Women’s rights movements
  • Youth rights movements
  • High-risk civic actors
  • Communities affected by humanitarian emergencies
  • People facing digital exclusion or online threats

Professional associations and regional technical bodies

Professional associations and specialized regional institutions may submit an EOI where they can contribute technical knowledge, standards, training systems, practitioner networks or policy support.

These may include associations or technical bodies working in social services, public health, law, justice, policing, digital technology, data governance, cybersecurity, research, evaluation or other relevant areas.

Organizations of persons with disabilities

Organizations of persons with disabilities are expressly included among eligible entities.

Their participation can help ensure that TFGBV prevention and response systems are inclusive, accessible and designed around the rights and lived experiences of persons with disabilities.

Specialized private-sector entities

Private-sector entities may apply where appropriate, particularly when they possess specialized expertise in:

  • Technology-facilitated gender-based violence
  • Digital security
  • Online safety
  • Digital evidence
  • Information management
  • Safe technology development
  • Ethical technology design
  • Safety-by-design principles

Private-sector participation must be relevant to the workstream proposed and consistent with UNFPA’s rights-based, ethical and survivor-centred requirements.

Four Main TFGBV Workstreams

Organizations may express interest in one or more of four specialized pillars.

Pillar 1: Response

The response pillar focuses on strengthening services, systems and professional capacity to assist survivors of technology-facilitated gender-based violence safely, ethically and confidentially.

Training and capacity building

Selected partners may help adapt and roll out UNFPA’s global TFGBV training package for frontline responders.

Target participants may include professionals working in:

  • Social work
  • Health services
  • Justice systems
  • Police and law enforcement
  • Local government
  • Gender-based violence services
  • Humanitarian response

Training may be implemented across priority countries, including humanitarian contexts.

Partners may also support the revision of national gender-based violence referral mechanisms so that they adequately incorporate survivor-centred responses to technology-facilitated abuse.

This work is important because referral systems designed only for conventional forms of violence may not fully address digital evidence, online threats, data privacy, account security or the continuing relationship between online and offline harm.

TFGBV responder support network

UNFPA APRO intends to facilitate a regional support network for TFGBV responders.

The network would allow digital programmers, gender-based violence specialists and frontline responders to exchange:

  • Practical experience
  • Technical guidance
  • Response protocols
  • Lessons from implementation
  • Emerging risks
  • Promising practices
  • Referral approaches
  • Survivor-safety considerations

Qualified partners may be expected to help establish, coordinate or provide technical support to this network.

Regional survivor-support mechanism

The call includes the potential establishment of a regional mechanism providing safe, ethical and confidential services to survivors of technology-facilitated gender-based violence.

Organizations interested in this area should be able to demonstrate strong safeguards relating to:

  • Survivor confidentiality
  • Informed consent
  • Data protection
  • Referral management
  • Risk assessment
  • Ethical case handling
  • Staff training
  • Accessibility
  • Protection from retaliation or further harm

Digital forensics

Partners may be selected to develop or support digital-forensic protocols for TFGBV cases.

These protocols should ensure that the collection and storage of digital evidence are:

  • Safe
  • Ethical
  • Survivor-centred
  • Confidential
  • Technically reliable
  • Consistent with relevant legal and protection requirements

Digital evidence can be highly sensitive. Poor collection or storage practices may expose survivors to further harm, compromise confidentiality or weaken legal and institutional responses.

Safe digital and information-management systems

The response pillar also covers systems used by GBV service providers to manage digital information.

Partners may support the design or implementation of secure systems that protect data held by organizations managing TFGBV cases.

This may involve reinforcing survivor-centred information-management systems and helping service providers reduce risks such as unauthorized access, insecure storage, inappropriate information sharing or loss of sensitive case information.

Pillar 2: Prevention

The prevention pillar focuses on reducing the risk of TFGBV before harm occurs and strengthening the ability of vulnerable groups and frontline actors to operate safely in digital environments.

Resilience of high-risk actors

Selected partners may strengthen the digital security, safety and operational resilience of groups facing elevated online risks.

These groups may include:

  • Frontline responders
  • Women’s rights movements
  • Youth rights movements
  • Human rights and civic actors
  • Professionals managing sensitive survivor information

Relevant interventions could include training, risk assessment, security protocols, technical guidance and institutional digital-safety measures.

Diverting young people from harmful online subcultures

The call also seeks support for developing digital-literacy modules and targeted programmes intended to divert young people away from the manosphere and other harmful online subcultures.

This workstream requires organizations capable of developing prevention approaches that are:

  • Evidence-based
  • Age-appropriate
  • Gender-responsive
  • Culturally relevant
  • Suitable for digital delivery
  • Focused on long-term behavioural and social change

Applicants proposing work under this area should clearly explain their experience with young people, online cultures, gender norms, digital literacy or violence-prevention programming.

Pillar 3: Enabling Environment

The enabling-environment pillar addresses the laws, policies, technologies, standards and financial systems that influence how TFGBV is prevented and addressed.

Rights-based laws and policies

Partners may help strengthen the ability of governments and civil society organizations to develop and implement laws and policies addressing technology-facilitated gender-based violence.

Activities may include:

  • Specialized legal advice
  • Policy analysis
  • Drafting support
  • Peer learning
  • Mentorship
  • Institutional capacity development
  • Rights-based assessment of existing legislation

The focus should be on legal and policy approaches that protect survivors and promote rights without creating unintended harm, exclusion or excessive restrictions.

Digital governance and technology-industry standards

UNFPA APRO is also seeking expertise related to gender-responsive digital governance and technology-industry practices.

Partners may advocate for or support the implementation of:

  • Safety-by-design principles
  • Ethical technology development
  • Gender-responsive digital governance
  • Improved technology-sector standards
  • Safer digital products
  • Platform policies addressing TFGBV
  • Prevention measures built into technology before harm occurs

Safety by design means considering user protection and foreseeable risks during the design and development of digital services rather than responding only after abuse has occurred.

Sustainable financing

Organizations may contribute evidence and investment cases that influence national planning and budgeting.

The objective is to encourage governments to allocate dedicated resources for TFGBV prevention and response rather than treating technology-facilitated violence as an unfunded or temporary policy issue.

This work may involve:

  • Costing interventions
  • Developing financing cases
  • Budget analysis
  • Public-finance advocacy
  • Evidence-based investment proposals
  • Engagement with national planning institutions

Applicants interested in this component should be capable of connecting technical TFGBV programming with national planning, public budgets and sustainable financing mechanisms.

Pillar 4: Research, Data and Evaluation

The fourth pillar focuses on building the evidence, measurement systems and ethical monitoring frameworks needed to improve TFGBV programmes and policies.

Ethical monitoring and evaluation

Partners may support the development of monitoring and evaluation frameworks for TFGBV programmes.

These frameworks should contain appropriate:

  • Indicators
  • Means of verification
  • Data-collection methods
  • Progress measures
  • Outcome measures
  • Impact-assessment approaches
  • Ethical safeguards

Monitoring systems must be survivor-centred and should not create additional risks for survivors or expose confidential information.

The resulting evidence should help UNFPA and its partners assess implementation, understand impact and improve future programming and policy.

Evidence generation and knowledge sharing

Selected entities may generate, synthesize and share evidence concerning TFGBV prevention and response at:

  • Regional level
  • Subregional level
  • National level

This may include:

  • Trend analysis
  • Literature reviews
  • Comparative research
  • Programme evaluations
  • Policy research
  • Documentation of promising practices
  • Evidence briefs
  • Knowledge products
  • Regional learning initiatives

Statistical standards and prevalence measurement

The call includes support for developing statistical standards and testing methods for measuring the prevalence of technology-facilitated gender-based violence.

Organizations interested in this work should demonstrate expertise in areas such as survey design, gender statistics, research ethics, measurement, methodology development or sensitive-data collection.

Administrative-data systems

Partners may also help improve existing administrative-data systems by integrating variables that allow reported TFGBV cases to be identified and tracked.

This work must balance the need for accurate information with survivor safety, confidentiality, informed consent and responsible data governance.

Selection Criteria

UNFPA APRO will assess applicants against several technical and institutional requirements.

Legal registration

Applicants must be legally registered entities. Both not-for-profit organizations and, where appropriate, for-profit organizations may be considered.

Applicants should be prepared to provide official registration documents and evidence of their legal status.

Technical capacity

The organization must demonstrate proven experience and expertise in at least one of the four TFGBV pillars.

A strong submission should clearly explain:

  • The workstream or workstreams selected
  • Relevant previous projects
  • Technical methodologies used
  • Results achieved
  • Populations or institutions supported
  • Geographic coverage
  • Relevant publications, tools or training materials
  • The organization’s comparative advantage

Institutional capacity

Applicants must demonstrate the institutional ability to implement activities effectively.

UNFPA expects evidence of:

  • Established internal governance
  • Clear operational policies
  • Human-resource policies
  • Adequate staffing
  • Results-based management capacity
  • Robust monitoring and evaluation systems
  • Suitable implementation and reporting arrangements

Applicants should avoid making unsupported statements about institutional strength. Claims should be backed by policies, organizational structures, previous performance or concrete examples.

Financial capacity

Organizations must have experience managing funds and a sound financial-management system.

The required financial capacity includes:

  • Robust internal controls
  • Fraud-prevention policies
  • Established accounting procedures
  • Reliable financial reporting
  • Evidence of responsible fund management

The organization’s financial systems must be supported by recent unqualified audit reports, such as audit reports covering the last two years.

An unqualified audit opinion generally means that the external auditor concluded that the financial statements were presented fairly in all material respects under the relevant reporting framework.

Applicants whose latest audit reports contain qualifications should carefully assess whether they meet this criterion before applying.

Ethical standards

Potential partners must demonstrate their commitment to UNFPA policies and ethical standards.

Particular emphasis is placed on UNFPA’s zero-tolerance approach to sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment, commonly addressed through Prevention of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse requirements.

Applicants should be able to demonstrate appropriate safeguarding policies, reporting mechanisms, staff conduct requirements and accountability procedures.

Geographical relevance

The applicant must have a demonstrated track record of successfully managing and implementing programmes in Asia and the Pacific.

The announcement does not expressly require every applicant to be headquartered within the region. However, organizations must provide credible evidence of relevant implementation experience, partnerships, networks or operational capacity in Asia and the Pacific.

Required Expression of Interest Documents

Interested organizations must prepare an Expression of Interest containing the following elements.

1. Cover letter

The cover letter should identify:

  • The organization submitting the EOI
  • The selected area or areas of interest
  • The relevant TFGBV pillar or pillars
  • How the organization aligns with the UNFPA Asia-Pacific Regional Programme
  • Why the organization is interested in collaborating with UNFPA APRO
  • The organization’s principal comparative advantage

The cover letter should be direct, evidence-based and tailored to the specific call.

2. Evidence of technical expertise

Applicants must provide evidence of their technical capacity and record of similar work.

Relevant evidence may include:

  • Previous project descriptions
  • Completion reports
  • Evaluation findings
  • Research publications
  • Training materials
  • Policies or technical tools developed
  • Contracts or partnership agreements
  • Examples of survivor-centred services
  • Digital-safety programmes
  • Advocacy outcomes
  • Relevant organizational profiles

Experience working with United Nations agencies is considered an advantage, although the announcement does not state that previous UN experience is mandatory.

3. Evidence of partnerships and networks

Applicants must explain their partnerships, networks and comparative advantage within Asia and the Pacific.

This may include relationships with:

  • Government institutions
  • Civil society networks
  • Women-led organizations
  • Youth-led organizations
  • Research institutions
  • Technology-sector actors
  • Survivor-service providers
  • Organizations of persons with disabilities
  • Regional bodies
  • International development partners

The submission should explain how these networks would help the applicant deliver regional, subregional or national-level work.

4. Proposed modalities of engagement

Applicants must indicate how they propose to collaborate with UNFPA APRO.

Possible modalities identified in the call include:

  • Technical support
  • Research
  • Advocacy
  • Innovation
  • Service delivery

Organizations may propose more than one modality where this is consistent with their capacity and the selected workstream.

A strong proposal should explain the proposed approach, expected contribution, institutional role and how the organization would coordinate with UNFPA and other partners.

How to Submit the UNFPA APRO Expression of Interest

Applications must be submitted by email.

Submission email: apro.office@unfpa.org

Required subject line: EOI on TFGBV

Application deadline: 14 August 2026

Applicants should ensure that the correct subject line is used so the submission can be properly identified and routed.

Before sending the email, organizations should verify that:

  1. The cover letter is signed by an authorized representative.
  2. The selected TFGBV workstream is clearly identified.
  3. All supporting evidence is attached.
  4. The organization’s legal name and contact information are accurate.
  5. Attached files open correctly.
  6. File names are clear and professionally formatted.
  7. The email is sent to the correct address.
  8. A copy of the complete submission is retained.
  9. Delivery confirmation or an email record is preserved.

The official announcement does not specify a required application portal or closing time. Applicants should therefore follow the stated email-submission process and send their EOI before the deadline date.

Funding and Partnership Arrangements

The call does not publish a fixed grant amount, budget ceiling or guaranteed level of financial support.

Eligible entities selected through the process will enter into an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO. Subject to funding availability, UNFPA APRO will then coordinate with successful implementing partners to agree on a work plan and establish a funded collaboration.

Organizations should therefore understand the process as involving two stages:

  1. Identification of technically and institutionally qualified partners through the Expression of Interest.
  2. Development of an agreed work plan and funded collaboration, subject to available resources.

Submission of an EOI does not itself guarantee selection, funding or a specific financial award.

Practical Tips for Preparing a Strong Submission

Select only relevant workstreams

Applicants should not claim expertise across all four pillars unless they can provide substantial evidence.

A focused application demonstrating deep experience in one or two areas may be more credible than a broad application containing unsupported claims.

Connect experience directly to TFGBV

General experience in gender equality, gender-based violence or information technology may not be sufficient on its own.

Applicants should clearly demonstrate how their experience relates to the interaction between digital technology and gender-based violence.

Demonstrate survivor-centred practice

Organizations proposing response, data, forensic or information-management activities should explain how they protect:

  • Safety
  • Privacy
  • Confidentiality
  • Dignity
  • Informed consent
  • Accessibility
  • Survivor decision-making

Provide evidence, not promotional language

Statements such as “we are a leading organization” or “we have extensive experience” should be supported by:

  • Project dates
  • Countries covered
  • Funding managed
  • Outputs delivered
  • People or institutions reached
  • Evaluations completed
  • Policies influenced
  • Systems established

Review financial documentation early

Because recent unqualified audit reports are specifically mentioned, applicants should confirm the availability and status of their latest external audits before preparing the complete EOI.

Explain regional value

Applicants should show how their proposed contribution would be relevant beyond a single isolated activity.

This may include regional networks, transferable tools, multilingual capacity, cross-country experience, policy connections or the ability to adapt interventions for different Asia-Pacific contexts.

Submit before 14 August 2026

The official notice does not specify a closing hour. Applicants should not wait until the final day, especially where internal approvals or large supporting documents are involved. APPLY HERE.

Conclusion

The UNFPA APRO Expression of Interest presents a significant institutional collaboration opportunity for organizations working at the intersection of gender-based violence, technology, digital safety, survivor services, public policy, research, data and human rights.

Through the call, UNFPA seeks partners capable of contributing to four major areas: survivor-centred response, prevention, an enabling legal and technological environment, and research, data and evaluation.

Eligible organizations must demonstrate legal registration, relevant technical expertise, institutional governance, sound financial systems, appropriate ethical standards and a record of programme implementation in Asia and the Pacific.

Successful entities may enter into an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO and subsequently develop a funded work plan, subject to the availability of resources.

Organizations considering an application should identify the workstream that best matches their expertise, assemble strong technical and financial evidence, explain their regional comparative advantage and submit the complete Expression of Interest to apro.office@unfpa.org using the subject line “EOI on TFGBV” no later than 14 August 2026
